title: Definitions
content: Key definitions: (1) 'claim' means right to payment or remedy for breach of contract; (2) 'corporate applicant' means corporate debtor, financial creditor, or operational creditor; (3) 'corporate debtor' means corporate person who owes debt to any person; (4) 'corporate person' means company, LLP, or other body incorporated with limited liability; (5) 'creditor' means person to whom debt is owed including financial creditor, operational creditor, secured creditor, unsecured creditor; (6) 'debt' means liability or obligation in respect of claim which is due from any person; (7) 'default' means non-payment of debt when whole or any part or instalment has become due and payable; (8) 'financial creditor' means person to whom financial debt is owed; (9) 'financial debt' means debt along with interest disbursed against consideration for time value of money including loans, bonds, debentures, lease or hire purchase where lessor/owner has transferred substantially all risks, receivables discounting, and credit facilities; (10) 'operational creditor' means person to whom operational debt is owed including dues for goods, services, employment, and government dues.

title: Applicability to Corporate Persons
content: Part II applies to: (a) any company incorporated under Companies Act 2013 or previous Acts; (b) any other company governed by special Act; (c) LLP under Limited Liability Partnership Act 2008; (d) such other body incorporated under any law with limited liability as Central Government may notify. Minimum default amount is Rs. 1 crore (raised from Rs. 1 lakh during COVID-19 and maintained thereafter). The Code does not apply to financial service providers except to the extent notified.

title: Definitions for Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process
content: (1) 'corporate insolvency resolution process' (CIRP) means insolvency resolution process for corporate persons under Chapter II; (2) 'creditors in a class' means creditors with at least 10% of total debt in that class; (3) 'financial creditor' means any person to whom a financial debt is owed; (4) 'financial debt' means debt disbursed against consideration for time value of money and includes: (a) money borrowed against interest; (b) credit facility; (c) amount under letters of credit/guarantee; (d) receivable or securitisation/discounting; (e) financial lease; (f) derivatives; (g) counter-indemnity; (h) amounts under indemnity; (5) 'insolvency commencement date' means date of admission of application; (6) 'insolvency resolution process period' means 180 days (extendable by 90 days, not exceeding 330 days total including extensions); (7) 'operational debt' means claim for provision of goods or services including employment, or debt for repayment of dues arising under any law for the time being in force.

title: Persons Who May Initiate CIRP
content: Where any corporate debtor commits a default, a financial creditor, an operational creditor, or the corporate debtor itself may initiate corporate insolvency resolution process in respect of such corporate debtor. The Code provides for creditor-in-control model where financial creditors through Committee of Creditors (CoC) drive the resolution process, replacing the erstwhile debtor-in-possession regime.

title: Initiation of CIRP by Financial Creditor
content: Financial creditor may file application before Adjudicating Authority (NCLT) for initiating CIRP against corporate debtor when default has occurred. Application to be in prescribed form with: (a) record of default from information utility or other evidence of default; (b) name of resolution professional proposed to act as interim resolution professional; (c) any other information. NCLT shall within 14 days of receipt ascertain existence of default from records of information utility or on basis of other evidence furnished. If satisfied, NCLT shall admit application and communicate to corporate debtor, appoint interim RP. If not satisfied, NCLT shall reject application with reasons. Amendment (2019): homebuyers treated as financial creditors under Section 5(8)(f) proviso.

title: Insolvency Resolution by Operational Creditor
content: Operational creditor shall deliver demand notice of unpaid operational debt or copy of invoice demanding payment. If corporate debtor fails to pay or bring to notice of operational creditor any dispute or record of pendency of suit or arbitration within 10 days, operational creditor may file application for initiating CIRP. Notice must be in prescribed form. Dispute or pre-existing litigation on the debt is ground for rejection. After 2018 Amendment, withdrawal of application by operational creditor requires 90% CoC approval if made after admission.

title: Application for Initiation of CIRP by Operational Creditor
content: After expiry of 10 days from delivery of notice under Section 8, if operational creditor does not receive payment or notice of dispute, may file application with NCLT with: (a) copy of invoice/demand notice with proof of delivery; (b) affidavit that there is no notice of dispute or record of pendency of suit/arbitration; (c) copy of certificate from financial institutions showing unpaid debt; (d) other information. NCLT shall within 14 days admit application if complete and there is no notice of dispute or pre-existing dispute, or reject application if incomplete, debt is disputed, or fully paid. Pre-existing dispute is valid ground for rejection even if raised after demand notice (Mobilox Innovations principle).

title: Initiation of CIRP by Corporate Debtor
content: Corporate debtor may file application for initiating CIRP with NCLT. Application to include: (a) books of account and financial statements for 2 preceding years; (b) statement of affairs; (c) special resolution of shareholders (for company) or resolution of partners (for LLP); (d) proposed IRP's name. NCLT shall within 14 days admit if application complete, or reject if incomplete. This is voluntary insolvency filing when debtor anticipates default or wishes to restructure. Controversial due to potential misuse - some debtors use Section 10 to escape operational creditors' claims.

title: Time-limit for Completion of Insolvency Resolution Process
content: CIRP shall be completed within 180 days from insolvency commencement date. NCLT may extend period by maximum 90 days on application by resolution professional if instructed by CoC with 66% voting share. The resolution process shall mandatorily be completed within 330 days including any extension granted and time excluded by NCLT. Time excluded includes: (a) time for admission of application up to 14 days; (b) period of any stay granted by any court/tribunal; (c) COVID-19 moratorium period (March 25, 2020 to March 24, 2021).

title: Withdrawal of Application
content: NCLT may allow withdrawal of application admitted under Sections 7, 9, or 10 on application made by applicant with approval of 90% voting share of CoC. Before constitution of CoC, withdrawal may be permitted by NCLT if application is filed within 14 days of appointment of IRP. The 2018 Amendment inserted this section recognizing the possibility of settlement between parties even after admission of CIRP.

title: Moratorium
content: On insolvency commencement date, NCLT shall declare moratorium prohibiting: (a) institution or continuation of suits or proceedings against corporate debtor including execution of judgment; (b) transferring, encumbering, alienating or disposing of any asset of corporate debtor; (c) recovery of property by owner or lessor where property is occupied by corporate debtor; (d) recovery of security interest by secured creditor; (e) termination, suspension, or interruption of essential supplies (electricity, water, telecom) to corporate debtor. Supply of essential goods/services not to be terminated during moratorium. Moratorium does not apply to: (a) transactions notified by Central Government; (b) surety liability; (c) criminal proceedings; (d) third party guarantors. Amendment (2020): personal guarantors' liability continues even during moratorium.

title: Appointment of Interim Resolution Professional
content: NCLT shall appoint Interim Resolution Professional (IRP) proposed by financial creditor or by IBBI if no proposal. IRP need not be from the same location as registered office of corporate debtor. IRP must confirm consent before appointment. IRP shall submit acceptance to NCLT within 7 days. NCLT to appoint another IRP if proposed IRP declines. IRP to make public announcement within 3 days of appointment calling for submission of claims.

title: Management of Affairs of Corporate Debtor by IRP
content: From insolvency commencement date: (a) management of affairs of corporate debtor vests in IRP; (b) powers of Board of Directors suspended and exercised by IRP; (c) officers and managers report to IRP and provide access to documents and records; (d) promoters, directors, employees to extend all assistance and cooperation to IRP. IRP acts as custodian of assets. IRP's management continues until either resolution professional is appointed under Section 22 or replaced by another IRP. This marks the shift from debtor-in-possession to creditor-in-control model.

title: Duties of Interim Resolution Professional
content: IRP shall: (a) collect all information relating to assets, finances, operations of corporate debtor for determining financial position as on insolvency commencement date; (b) receive and collate claims submitted by creditors; (c) constitute Committee of Creditors (CoC); (d) monitor assets of corporate debtor and manage operations as going concern; (e) file information collected with information utility; (f) take control and custody of assets over which corporate debtor has ownership rights (recorded in balance sheet or with information utility or otherwise); (g) perform such other duties as may be specified.

title: Management of Operations of Corporate Debtor as Going Concern
content: IRP shall make every endeavor to protect and preserve value of property of corporate debtor and manage operations as going concern. IRP has authority to: (a) appoint accountants, legal/other professionals required; (b) enter into contracts on behalf of corporate debtor or amend/modify existing contracts for keeping corporate debtor as going concern; (c) raise interim finance but not create security interest over any encumbered property without prior consent of creditors whose debt is secured by such property (consent required is 66% in value if secured creditors form part of CoC); (d) issue instructions to personnel of corporate debtor for keeping it as going concern; (e) take all necessary actions in compliance with applicable laws.

title: Committee of Creditors
content: IRP shall constitute CoC after collation of claims received against corporate debtor and verification thereof. CoC shall comprise all financial creditors of corporate debtor. Where financial debt is in form of securities held by depository, the trustee shall be member of CoC. Operational creditors, if aggregate dues >= 10% of total debt, may attend meetings but without voting rights. Government treated as financial creditor where it has given guarantee to financial debt. Related party financial creditors present but do not vote. CoC voting share calculated based on financial debt owed. CoC may include workmen and employees' provident fund and pension fund dues as financial debt (Amendment 2018).

title: Appointment of Resolution Professional
content: First meeting of CoC to be held within 7 days of constitution of CoC. CoC in first meeting may, by majority vote of not less than 66% voting share, either resolve to appoint the IRP as Resolution Professional, or propose another Insolvency Professional (IP) to act as RP. NCLT shall forward name to IBBI for confirmation. IBBI may reject within 10 days on grounds of disciplinary proceedings pending or conditions specified by regulations. If IBBI rejects, CoC shall propose another IP within 10 days.

title: Duties of Resolution Professional
content: RP shall: (a) take immediate custody and control of all assets of corporate debtor including business records; (b) represent and act on behalf of corporate debtor with third parties, exercise rights for benefit of corporate debtor in judicial, quasi-judicial, arbitration proceedings; (c) raise interim finance (subject to approval of CoC with 66% voting share); (d) appoint accountants, legal and other professionals; (e) maintain updated list of claims; (f) convene and attend all CoC meetings; (g) prepare information memorandum; (h) invite prospective resolution applicants (PRAs) to submit resolution plans; (i) present all resolution plans to CoC; (j) file application for avoidance of transactions if any.

title: Approval of CoC for Certain Actions
content: RP shall not, without prior CoC approval (66% voting share): (a) raise any interim finance in excess of Rs. 10 crore; (b) create any security interest over assets of corporate debtor; (c) change capital structure including by way of further issue of securities; (d) record change in ownership interest of corporate debtor; (e) give instructions to financial institutions to make any transfers from accounts of corporate debtor; (f) undertake any related party transaction; (g) amend any constitutional document of corporate debtor; (h) delegate powers of RP to any other person; (i) dispose of or permit disposal of shares of any shareholder of corporate debtor or their nominees.

title: Persons Not Eligible to Be Resolution Applicant
content: A person shall not be eligible to submit resolution plan if such person or connected person: (a) is undischarged insolvent; (b) is willful defaulter in accordance with RBI guidelines; (c) has account classified as NPA for 1+ year and is unable to settle overdue amounts including interest/charges (exceptions for financial entities bidding for financial sector assets); (d) has been convicted for offence punishable with imprisonment of 2+ years and 2 years not elapsed from date of release; (e) is disqualified to act as director under Companies Act; (f) is prohibited by SEBI from trading/accessing securities markets; (g) has been promoter or in management of corporate debtor when account became NPA and at least 1 year has not elapsed from such date; (h) has executed guarantee in favour of creditor for corporate debtor against which CIRP is pending; (i) has been subject of a suspension order under Section 19 of IBBI; (j) has been subject of a direction under Section 74(2); (k) is connected person defined to include promoters, directors, relatives, related parties, and persons with joint ventures or joint control. The 2018 Amendment clarified that any prospective resolution applicant disqualified under Section 29A is ineligible for any resolution plan and cannot acquire stressed assets.

title: Submission of Resolution Plan
content: Resolution applicant may submit resolution plan to RP within prescribed timeline. Resolution plan shall provide for: (a) payment of insolvency resolution process costs in a specified manner in priority to repayment of other debts; (b) payment of debts of operational creditors which shall not be less than: (i) amount payable in liquidation value or (ii) amount receivable under resolution plan by dissenting financial creditors, whichever is higher; (c) payment of debts of financial creditors in accordance with provisions of this Act; (d) implementation and supervision of resolution plan; (e) provision for effective means of monitoring implementation of plan; (f) does not contravene any provision of law for the time being in force; (g) conforms to such other requirements as may be specified. Resolution plan must include statement of manner in which resolution applicant dealt with interests of all stakeholders including operational creditors.

title: Approval of Resolution Plan
content: If CoC approves resolution plan by not less than 66% voting share, RP shall submit resolution plan to NCLT. NCLT may, within 30 days of filing, approve resolution plan which shall be binding on all stakeholders including Central Government, State Government, local authority, and guarantor (even if not party to resolution plan). If NCLT is satisfied that resolution plan does not contravene any of the provisions of law and conforms to Section 30(2) requirements, it shall by order approve the resolution plan. NCLT may reject resolution plan if it is not in compliance with Section 30(2) or provisions of law. Resolution plan shall be implemented from the date of order approving it. The term 'binding on all stakeholders' includes governmental authorities meaning tax/statutory dues are extinguished as per the resolution plan (Ghanshyam Mishra & Sons principle - Supreme Court 2021).

title: Initiation of Liquidation
content: Where NCLT does not receive resolution plan within the period specified or CoC decides by 66% voting share at any time during CIRP to liquidate corporate debtor or resolution plan is contravened by concerned corporate debtor and any person affected thereby makes application to NCLT, NCLT shall pass liquidation order. Order of liquidation shall be deemed to be notice of discharge to officers, employees, and workmen except when business continued during liquidation. Liquidation order shall be published and communicated in manner specified. RP shall act as Liquidator for liquidation unless replaced by NCLT. Corporate debtor ceases to exist upon dissolution following liquidation.

title: Preferential Transactions and Relevant Time
content: A corporate debtor is deemed to have given preference if: (a) there is a transfer of property or interest thereof of corporate debtor for benefit of a creditor or surety or guarantor; (b) such transfer has effect of putting such creditor, surety, or guarantor in a more beneficial position than it would have been in if such transfer had not been made. 'Relevant time' means: (a) within 2 years preceding insolvency commencement date, if preference given to related party; (b) within 1 year preceding insolvency commencement date, in any other case. RP/Liquidator may apply to NCLT for avoidance of preferential transaction. Defenses: (a) transfer was made in ordinary course of business; (b) transfer created security interest to secure new value.

title: Avoidance of Undervalued Transactions
content: Undervalued transaction means: (a) a gift made by corporate debtor; or (b) a transaction where corporate debtor received consideration significantly less than the value of the transaction. RP or Liquidator may apply to NCLT to declare transaction void if: (a) transaction is undervalued; (b) it was made within 1 year before insolvency commencement date (2 years for related party); (c) corporate debtor was insolvent at time of transaction or became insolvent as a result of the transaction. NCLT may: (a) require beneficiary to pay sum to RP/Liquidator; (b) require property transferred to be vested in corporate debtor; (c) release or discharge any security interest.

title: Distribution of Assets (Waterfall Mechanism)
content: The proceeds from sale of liquidation assets shall be distributed in the following order of priority: (a) insolvency resolution process costs and liquidation costs paid in full; (b) workmen's dues for 24 months preceding liquidation commencement date AND debts owed to secured creditor who has relinquished security interest (to be paid in pari passu manner); (c) wages and unpaid dues owed to employees other than workmen for 12 months preceding liquidation; (d) financial debts owed to unsecured creditors; (e) government dues (central, state, local) and debts owed to secured creditor for any unpaid dues following enforcement of security interest (remaining dues after enforcement paid in pari passu manner); (f) remaining debts and dues; (g) preference shareholders if any; (h) equity shareholders or partners. Note: Secured creditors can either relinquish security (rank at (b)) or realize outside liquidation (rank at (e) for shortfall). Government dues rank below unsecured financial creditors.

title: Dissolution of Corporate Debtor
content: Where assets of corporate debtor have been completely liquidated and no further proceedings required, Liquidator shall apply to NCLT for dissolution of corporate debtor. NCLT shall pass order of dissolution within 14 days of receipt of application. From date of order, corporate debtor shall stand dissolved and its name struck off from records of registering authority. Dissolution order to be forwarded to concerned Registrar of Companies.

title: Adjudicating Authority for Corporate Persons
content: NCLT shall be Adjudicating Authority for purposes of insolvency resolution and liquidation for corporate persons. Where CIRP or liquidation proceeding relates to corporate debtor who is a personal guarantor to such corporate debtor or in insolvency resolution of such personal guarantor, proceedings relating to guarantor shall be filed before NCLT dealing with CIRP or liquidation of corporate debtor. Appeal against NCLT orders lies to NCLAT within 30 days (extendable by 15 days). Appeal from NCLAT to Supreme Court within 45 days.

title: Appeals and Appellate Authority
content: Any person aggrieved by order of NCLT may prefer appeal to NCLAT within 30 days from date of order. NCLAT may extend period by maximum 15 days if satisfied that appellant was prevented by sufficient cause from filing appeal in time. No appeal shall lie from order approving resolution plan except on following grounds: (a) resolution plan is in contravention of provisions of any law; (b) there has been material irregularity in exercise of powers by RP during CIRP; (c) debts owed to operational creditors have not been provided for in manner specified; (d) resolution plan does not comply with other requirements specified.

title: Fraudulent Trading and Wrongful Trading
content: If during CIRP or liquidation, it is found that business of corporate debtor has been carried on with intent to defraud creditors or for fraudulent purpose, NCLT may direct that persons who were knowingly parties to carrying on the business shall be liable to make such contributions to assets of corporate debtor as NCLT may deem fit. Additionally, where corporate debtor has traded beyond point at which director/partner knew or ought to have known that there was no reasonable prospect of avoiding insolvency and failed to take steps to minimize potential loss to creditors, the director/partner shall be liable to make such contribution as NCLT deems fit. This section introduces wrongful trading (UK concept) which creates director liability even without intent to defraud.

title: Application to Micro, Small and Medium Enterprises
content: Provisions relating to pre-packaged insolvency resolution process under Chapter III-A shall apply to MSMEs. Section 29A (eligibility criteria) shall not apply to resolution applicant in respect of CIRP of MSMEs (as per Section 240A(1)). This relaxation enables promoters of MSMEs to bid for their own companies. However, this exemption is not applicable if promoter is wilful defaulter or disqualified director. The pre-packaged insolvency resolution process (PPIRP) for MSMEs was introduced in April 2021 as a debtor-friendly fast-track mechanism for distressed but viable MSMEs.

title: Personal Insolvency Framework
content: Part III applies to individuals and partnership firms. Fresh start process (Sections 80-93) for individuals with gross annual income <= Rs. 60,000, assets <= Rs. 20,000, qualifying debts <= Rs. 35,000, not owner of dwelling, eligible for discharge of qualifying debts. Insolvency resolution process for individuals (Sections 94-120) when debtor commits default of Rs. 1,000 or more. Bankruptcy process for individuals (Sections 121-144) initiated when insolvency resolution fails or bankrupt estate inadequate. Personal guarantors to corporate debtors covered under Sections 95-187 with separate notification. Part III (except personal guarantors provisions) not yet fully notified as of 2024 for non-corporate guarantors.
